story:
Teddy woke to a bright spill of morning sun slipping through his curtains and across his face, the glow-in-the-dark stars on his ceiling fading into white. He had a tidy plan: up, brush teeth, get dressed, slippers, breakfast, then finish the last painting in his new collection. The plan vanished the moment his eyes adjusted and found a man in the corner of his room.

The man was impossibly tall, so tall he had to crook his neck to fit beneath the ceiling. He was thin and drooping in a dusty, tattered suit, with a long, pale, wrinkled face and a frown wide enough to span the top of Teddy’s head. Teddy hugged his blanket. “What are you doing here?” he asked.

The man did not answer, only turned his head in a stiff jolt that made Teddy start and back into the wall. When a voice finally came, it was a low croak from somewhere inside the man; the mouth didn’t so much speak as ripple like curtains in a strong gust. “Don’t mind me,” he said.

“I most certainly will mind you,” Teddy shot back. “You’re in my bedroom. Please leave.”

“I’ve been here as long as you have,” the man said, and then he slid into the next corner with a long, spidery motion, re-forming the same stooped pose, his gaze never leaving Teddy. He never stepped into the center or set foot along a flat wall; he touched down only where two planes met. He clung to corners like a nervous guest clings to a doorway.

Teddy edged toward the door, but the man skimmed sideways and claimed the ceiling corner beside it, one long hand dangling over the frame like a claw. “Oh, were you leaving?” the man murmured, feigning innocence. Teddy could tell he knew exactly what he was doing.

“Why do you keep switching corners?” Teddy asked. The man only stared.

That stare pushed Teddy from scared to stubborn. He yanked out his drying rack and shoved it on squeaking wheels across the floor toward the corner the man had vacated. The instant it neared, the man blurred—more reflex than decision—and reassembled above the door.

Now he looked angry. His frown sagged lower. His arms stretched with a sudden lunge, hands clawing for Teddy. Teddy scrambled away. The man flung himself into yet another corner, reforming, reaching again. Teddy hurled his blanket and pillow into a different corner, and the man jerked away as if the objects themselves barred him. The long arms flopped and then snapped back to his sides like the blade of his father’s retracting tape measure.

Teddy understood: the man could exist only in bare corners.

He wrestled his bed toward the remaining space, shoving, sweating. Each time he pushed, the man tried to anticipate, to reach, to catch him. “What are you?” Teddy asked between heaves.

“I am the thing in the corner of your room,” the man said. “I live where light doesn’t go. You left the curtains open, boy, and the shadows cut deep. I remain here. And I intend to stay.”

Teddy feinted toward the door to lure him, sprang back, and made the final shove. The bed kissed the wall. Only one bare corner remained now—the one the man already occupied above the door—and with every other corner blocked, he had nowhere left to flee. The man, stranded above the door, thrashed, his frown drooping like chewed gum. Teddy drew a breath, leapt with both fists raised like a warrior—and before he touched him, the man unraveled into mist. Poof. Gone.

Teddy stood in the wreck of his victorious morning, a battlefield of blankets, bed frame, and sunlit walls. He had guarded his room, won back his plan, and decided it was time for breakfast.
